speaker: interviewer
text: Doing a tape number 5 in an interview with Katherine Sattler. OK, so we left off, you're in Bratislava, you-- a man had approached you and asked you if you were, you're Andy's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Andy's sister. And I said, yes. And he says, he's alive. And I said, how wonderful, and then where is he. He's in Prague. I says, where is he. He's in one of the hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- naturally, we were thrilled. I went back to, we went back to Prague. My mother, my sister went back into the hospital, where they belonged at that time. And I set out early in the morning the next day to try to find my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now they set up hospitals in, also in schools, you know, temporary hospitals. And there was so many that there was no way of calling and finding out whether, what hospital he's at. It was just absolutely impossible. But they told me what schools were converted into hospitals to go and look for. So I set out very early in the morning and I start to walk in Prague, just one street after the other. And I went-- it was like 4 o'clock in the afternoon and I was very exhausted, very tired. And I went to several hospitals on, like within a two block radius. And then I came upon the last. I figured, this is the last one. I mean, I can't walk any longer. I mean, I'm just totally exhausted. I walked in and again I asked, do you have anybody by the name of Andrew, Andrew Rosenbaum registered. And the girl at the desk says, no, we don't. I said, OK, thank you, I walked out. And in Europe, they have the buildings very low like. They-- the schools, the schools are situated like you can look out onto the street, it's street level. The windows are street level. So there were two windows or three windows open. The first window I walked by, it was open. I looked in and I saw two guys talking. One guy was shaved, you know, bald headed. And the other one was talking to him. And I just walked by and I passed it up. And I looked in the other one and I paid no attention. Then as I went to the third window and something in my mind said, start all over again, go back. I went back to the first window and I was standing there and they were talking. And I was standing and I just said, Andy. And he turned around, and it was my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I cannot begin to tell you [LAUGHS] the reunion that I had with him. It was unbelievable. And I walked in and he told me what was the problem with him. He had thrombosis of the leg. He almost amputate his leg. And I said to him, gee, Andy, I says, you don't look so bad, you know. You have a nice round face. I said, why don't you ask me about [HUNGARIAN]. And he says, I don't want to. I said, well, well, you should because she's here. Well, we both cried naturally. And as I was talking to him, I wanted him to kind of quiet down a little bit. And I said to him, Andy, why don't you ask me about, about [HUNGARIAN], which means mother in Hungarian. He says, I don't want to. I says, [HUNGARIAN] is here, too. And that was the happiest day in our lives, [SOBBING] in my brother's and in my life. And from then on, I made arrangements for the doctor to transfer my brother into the same hospital as my mother and my sister were. And they were there for a while. And one day I was in a Red Cross kitchen because I was in a hospital-- they were in a hospital. I was in a Red Cross kitchen. And my mother says, you know something, I'll go with you. They'll let me get out of the hospital for a few hours. I will. I says, good idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came with me. And we were standing in line for food at a Red Cross kitchen. And there was an American soldier. And he was kind of looking around. And, and he spoke a very wonderful Hungarian. And I-- we asked him who is he looking for. And he told us he's looking for his cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start to talk to him and my mother says, well, where are you from. And he says, you mean, where do I live now. She says, yeah. He says, in Los Angeles. And my mother says, in Los Angeles. I have two brothers in Los Angeles. And he says, well, do you know where they live. She says, no. I, I, naturally, I'd give you their names, but I don't know where they live. He says to my mother, I want you here tomorrow at the same time, which is 12 o'clock noon. I want you back in the same place at 12 o'clock and I'm going to bring some news from your brother, hopefully. And sure enough, next day we were standing in the Red Cross kitchen, not for food anymore. We were just hoping to hear and see him. And he walks in and he says, I have good news for you, I reached both of your brothers. And they are going to send some affidavits and some letters to the American Embassy. And if you have a chance, please go down to the American Embassy and they'll give you all the instructions as to what to do from now on. And that was our beginning of getting out of Czechoslovakia into the United States. After that, we started things wrong, when we registered, we were there-- my brother, and my brother was very sick still, he was not well. But he was willing to go down to the American Embassy. We were sleeping outside the American Embassy like 3 o'clock in the morning. And because they didn't open until 8:00, 8:30. And we were the very first ones at the gate. We would not move for anything in the world. And we were there until they opened the gates. And we went in and we told them why we were there. And we told them that somebody got in touch with our uncles through the Red Cross in Los Angeles, and he was going to send word about what to do and how to go about whatever he intends to do. And we, I registered my mother, I registered my sister and myself. And also at that time, already my little cousin that I found in Auschwitz was with us. We found him in Prague. So he joined us again. And there were five of us by then. And we registered. And they told-- the Red Cross again told us, if we want to have a after my brother and my sister and my mother get out of the hospital, if we want to have an apartment, the only place there is is in [CZECH], which is not too far from Prague. And naturally, we were thrilled. We moved to [CZECH]. We were there maybe about four or five months. In the meantime, we were going into a Prague to process our pictures, whatever had to be done. And the, the, the passport and all the papers that had to be taken care of. And it wasn't too far off from that time that we were able to get out of Prague and, and, and come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what date you arrived in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: What time did we arrive to United?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What day? Oh yes, we arrived-- I'll never forget because the beautiful sight of the Statue of the Liberty, well, I'll always remember. We arrived July 22, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you arrived in New York, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to New York. And I have a cousin, my mother has a cousin that lives in Passaic, New Jersey that picked us up from the, from the-- not the airport-- at the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dock.

speaker: interviewee
text: The dock. Thank you. From the dock. And of course, I never saw them, they never saw us. But we told them we're the five of us. And they, they-- when we registered at the, at the immigration, they asked somebody if a five-member family comes through, and there are two girl-- and three ladies and two boys, to let them know. And that's what they did. And that's how they found us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long before you went out to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us-- first of all, they took us out for a wonderful dinner. [LAUGHS] I was sick like a dog on the ship anyway for 12 days. It took us 12 days. By the way, we went from-- from Prague, we went to Gothenburg, which is Sweden because we, that was our point of embarkation is Swedenburg-- I mean, Sweden, Gothenburg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us to a kosher restaurant, which at that time didn't mean that much to us because we ate anything that was available in Czechoslovakia-- horse meat and pork, and whatever there was we ate. When, then they took us to Lake Peekskill. They had like a summer home. So they took us there for a few days. And from there, they put us on a train from, from New York. And by train, we went to Los Angeles by train. It took us, I think, two nights or three nights, whatever it was, I can't remember. But I know we had a wonderful time. Being free and being in the United States was the most beautiful feeling in the world, knowing that we are free, and such a beautiful land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you, you arrive in LA?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles, my mother had her two brothers. They waited for us and they, they naturally, they were thrilled to have us. And they, they put us up in their apartment. They, actually they gave us their apartment and they moved away from the apartment. And we occupied their apartment. And that's where we, we lived for a while. I went to work right away, naturally, because we had to make a living. And, and I had a pretty good trade in my hand. So I knew that I'll be able to make some money and support ourselves. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What trade was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- actually, somebody called, I was not a dressmaker, I was in a dressmaking business. I was a designer and I started to work for a very lovely company. And eventually, I worked with Edith Head. And I had a very good job and everybody in my family was working, except my mother. I would not let my mother work. I said, not you. Everybody else can work, not you. You don't deserve it, you have to stay home and take care of your family. And my brother had a wonderful job. He was a baker, a pastry baker. He got a fabulous job in Los Angeles. So he was making good money. My sister went to work for my uncle, who was a dress manufacturer. So my sister ended up working for my, my uncle. And we were there two years. And I met my husband and we got married in Los Angeles. And my husband is a Detroiter. And he decided to come back home to Detroit. And I have been here ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children. We have two sons and a daughter. The oldest is a son, and the middle is a daughter, and the youngest is a son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have five grandchildren. My daughter has two children, a boy and a girl. My youngest son has two little girls and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, then, is there anything else you'd like to add, anything that I missed you'd like to, to say at the end here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm thrilled to be here in the United States. And I want to thank Mr. Spielberg for giving me this opportunity to-- to tell my story, which was, to me, it was a little bit hard to start with. But I'm glad that I did it. I want the world to know what went on before the-- before the war, during the war, and after the war. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. Thank you. OK, Miss Sattler, would you like to introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to introduce my husband of 46 years. We've been married for 46 years. His name is Carl Sattler. And he's lived here most of his life in the Detroit area. And we are, we are very happily married. And we are proud parents of three beautiful children. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how about a few grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. And, naturally, I have five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, can you tell me what this picture is of, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture is, was taken at my mother's wedding. And unfortunately, all the people there are in this picture are not alive today. The first row of the ladies that you see in the picture went away in the, in the gas chamber. They were in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- my mother got married in 1920, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how did you get possession of this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle who lived here in Los Angeles must have had this picture and he turned it over to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you explain this for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this picture represents my whole family, was my whole family. And this was taken some times in the 19-- early 1930s. Also, I got this picture from my uncle in Los Angeles, who had it in his possession.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so on the bottom two are your parents. When, when did your mother pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother passed away here in 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was taken away. The first day he entered Auschwitz he died. Because as I said, I found his clothes. My sister died in 1986. She's the one that was in a camp with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she is behind your father in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is behind my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother is-- lives in Los Angeles. And he's very happily married and a wonderful grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Ungvar, where I lived. It's called Uzhhorod, naturally. And this was a very beautiful synagogue, an old synagogue that was frequented naturally by my parents and myself. And it was very orthodox. The ladies were separate on the upper level, and the men were on the bottom level. And it was a very beautifully-built old synagogue. I cherish the thought that it's still existing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was taken in Prague at the Red Cross kitchen where my mother naturally had her meals from, and my brother. That was during the time when he was very sick and my mother was very sick. They just got together one day just to have some fun out of the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I don't even know how it got to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken of my mother, my sister, and myself showing our consecutive tattoos. My sister's number was 6731, my mother's 6732, and mine is 6733. My sister was the one who insisted on taking a picture because she felt that it was very important to-- for us to show the world. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This shows my tattoo on my arm, which is A-6733. And that's my tattoo that I got in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does the A stand for, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea what the A-- A stands for probably the [GERMAN] because that's where we were in [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. And I just wanted to show everybody that, yes, it did really exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ok. OK, can you describe what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at my brother, my youngest brother's wedding. And from left to right, it's my youngest brother Ronald and my husband Carl Sattler. I'm in the middle. My daughter Janis Fried and my son Howard Sattler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, this is at your youngest son's wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was at my youngest son's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that is on the right side-- on the left side. [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest granddaughter. Her name is Courtney Sattler. And she is quite a little gal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- already on, on this picture she was two and a half years old. But today, she is for four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter. Her name is the Ashley Sattler. And she is the second child, my son's, youngest son's. She's quite a little gal, very pretty little girl. We love her dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, I mean now she is six years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my little grandson. His name is Joshua Sattler. Today, he is nine years old. And he belongs to Ronald Sattler. He's quite a little guy. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] On the left side is my granddaughter Erin Fried. She belongs to my daughter Janice Fried. On the right side is David Fried. And Erin is 10 years old and David is eight years old. And they're quite wonderful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the video taping session. We used five tapes.
